items.q: How does the Brundtland Report define sustainable development?
items.correct: Meeting present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet theirs.
items.why: Students treat sustainability as 'no growth'. The Brundtland definition explicitly balances development AND environmental concerns - it's about INTERGENERATIONAL equity, not freezing change.

items.q: How are most microplastics formed in the environment?
items.correct: Larger plastic items break down physically into smaller fragments over time.
items.misconception: They are all manufactured at microscopic size deliberately.
items.why: Students think microplastics are designed small. Most are SECONDARY - formed when sun, waves, and wear break down larger items. Primary microplastics (microbeads, fibres) are a smaller proportion.

items.q: Why is atmospheric nitrogen unavailable to most plants directly?
items.correct: N2 has a strong triple bond that most organisms cannot break.
items.misconception: Because plants cannot reach high enough to access it.
items.why: Students miss the chemistry. Despite 78% of air being N2, the molecule's triple bond is energetically costly to break. Only specialised nitrogen-fixing bacteria (and lightning, industrial Haber process) can convert it to usable forms.

items.q: Who argued food can't keep up with population (1798)?
items.why: Correct answer: Malthus. “Darwin” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Shift from high to low birth/death rates is called?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Demographic transition. “Carrying capacity” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Exact answer: 8 billion. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
